-- 1 of 17 --

1. Surprise
2. Anticipation
3. Empathy
But it’s almost always a surprise. An
assemblage of words is constructed in
such a way as to make the totality of the
expression surprising to the listener.
What Makes Something Funny?
Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	02
But almost all of these surprises can be boiled down to contrast. Two
things are compared in such a way as to make the contrast between them
surprising to the listener.
Below are 5 of my 25 of my strategies to make people laugh, but think of
them more as strategic lessons on the same principle. Each represents a
different way to look at essentially the same thing:
Surprise.
As you learn to deploy these strategies, both in prepared content and
perhaps in the wild, please remember:
Everything is everything.
If you find yourself thinking that strategy 4 is simply a redressing of
strategy 5, I likely agree. If you think that strategy 1 and 2 are all driving
home the same point, you’re probably right. All of these strategies aim to
achieve the same basic effect, but by providing differing lenses on the
same concept, creativity is spurred, your chances of finding the places
where humor is hiding increase, and your opportunities to be funny
expand considerably.

-- 2 of 17 --

There’s more than one way to skin a cat, which is disgusting, not
because it evokes the image of the skinning of a cat (which is indeed
disgusting), but because it’s a cliché euphemism, and all clichés (and
almost all euphemisms) are disgusting because they have already been
spoken ten kabillion times.
But this cliché, in this particular case, is also true. We are trying to evoke
surprise, anticipation, and empathy in our audiences, but mostly, we are
trying to surprise them. There are many ways to do the same thing, just
like there are apparently many ways to skin a cat.
Also, by using this stupid, disgusting cliché, I get to tell you how stupid
clichés are, so it serves a useful purpose.
This is surprising since it’s so rare for a cliché to serve any useful
purpose.
So please don’t nitpick my strategies. Don’t be the person who says,
“Actually, definitions and metaphor are oftentimes the same thing.”
First, yes, we all know that’s true. They are almost identical. I just
acknowledged it. The idea is not exactly profound.
More importantly, nitpickers are never funny. They are reviled,
abhorred, and unliked by all. They never get laughs and probably have
very few friends.
Don’t be that person.
Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	03

-- 3 of 17 --

Where and When to be Funny?
Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	04
The first minute of a story.
Here's a fact that some people find hard to swallow:
You should not always try to be funny.
Funny people - people who know how to effectively deploy humor
regardless of the situation - understand that strategically locating humor is
far more effective than constantly being funny.
This means that for every joke I tell, I've probably left three jokes on the
cutting room floor.
Every time I make a group of people laugh at a dinner party, I've probably
kept my mouth shut half a dozen times, knowing that timing is everything.
People want to be funny all of the time. Those people are obnoxious.
Instead, funny people place your humor in the time and space where it can
serve you best.
Those places and times include:
That first laugh alters the brain chemistry of your audience by triggering
the release of dopamine, oxytocin, and endorphins in their brains.
Dopamine enhances learning, motivation, and attention.
Your audience will be primed to receive your message.
Oxytocin is considered the “empathy hormone” and the “bonding
chemical.” When it enters the bloodstream, it creates feelings of
relatedness and connection with the source of that release.
Your audience will instantly feel more connected to you.
Endorphins trigger feelings of pleasure. Your audience will feel better about
themselves and the world around them.

-- 4 of 17 --

Right before describing something terrible, painful, or traumatic, in
order to enhance the awfulness of the moment by contrasting it with
humor just before
Right after describing something terrible, painful, or traumatic, to afford
your audience - especially the more empathetic members - to take a
breath and reset
In the boring parts, to make them less boring
When there are no stakes in your story because a stakes-less story -
even for a few moments - can be ignored
Never at the end of a story.
The Big Sick
Jurassic Park
Breakfast Club
Forrest Gump
Terminator 2
Raiders of the Lost Ark
Die Hard
Stand By Me
We tell stories in an effort to say something, about ourselves, our company,
a product, a theory, a belief, a passion.
We undermine these efforts by ending with a joke, a pun, or a laugh of any
kind. Audiences LOVE to laugh, but they do not want to laugh in the final
seconds of a story. That precious real estate should be dedicated to moving
our audience emotionally, convincing them of universal truth, upending
their beliefs, changing their minds, or being vulnerable.
Want to see humor deployed strategically? Watch
the following films. When you laugh, ask yourself:
Why did the screenwriter/director include a joke here?
Where and When To Be Funny?
Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	05

Humor hides in the areas of greatest
optionality.
When you have a choice of what to say,
your chances of being funny increase
exponentially.
These areas include:
Where Humor Hides
Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	06
Humor is the hardest to achieve when you are trapped in the specificity
of plot, dialogue, data, and hard facts.
These areas inherently contain less optionality, so humor is more difficult
to achieve.
Not impossible, of course. Just harder.

Pitch
Pacing

Richard Pryor
Maria Bamford
Mitch Hedberg
George Carlin
Jerry Seinfeld
Tig Notaro
Excellent delivery is not essential but can make things that aren't inherently
funny amusing enough to produce a laugh.
Oftentimes it's the way you say something even more than what you say that
produces a laugh.
The means of doing this include:
Oftentimes a collection of words that are not inherently funny on their own
but can be said in such a way as to produce a laugh.
You must keep these four tools at the forefront of your mind when trying to
be funny because learning how and when a shift in volume, a perfectly
placed pause, an increase in pacing, or a shift in pitch can make something
special happen.
Especially practice this when speaking with friends and family, when
performance stakes are nonexistent.
You can also listen to the ways others use these tools in their comedy.
Here is a list of comedians who are especially effective at using these tools
to produce laughs:
Delivery
Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	07

-- 7 of 17 --

Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	08
Confidence
Confidence can't be taught but can be earned through experience, both on
the stage and in real life. The more you struggle and survive, the more fears
you choose to conquer, and the more often you defeat the undefeatable
enemy, the greater the confidence you will possess.
You must do hard things in life if you want to be a confident person.
You must do things that make you frightened, uncomfortable, and anxious.
Easy. Right?
But nothing will assist you in comedy (or life) more than feeling confident
when taking the stage.
Armor
You cannot allow an audience's failure to laugh at a joke to impact you in any
way, otherwise, your other jokes will likely fail, too.
Remember this at all times:
Comedy is not a 100% game. It's a fractional game.
Some jokes are supposed to fail. Your job is to identify the failures as quickly
as possible and eliminate them from your future while not allowing them to
impact the present.
When a joke fails to produce a laugh, it's simply data that can be used to
improve future performance.
It is not an indictment of you as a human being.
Four useful tools for assisting
with delivery include:

-- 8 of 17 --

Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	09
What did I do there?
Why was that funny?
Was it simply situationally funny or did I do something to make that laugh
happen?
Could I repeat that joke in the same way again?
Disdain
It's okay (and even helpful) to despise an audience that fails to laugh at a
proven laugh. If you know it's funny but the audience disagrees, hate them
for it.
That disdain will carry you forward unaffected by the audience's stupidity.
This should only apply when you know your joke is funny and has proven to
be funny in the past. This does not mean you should hate every audience that
fails to find you funny.
You might just be unfunny.
Your Ears
You must be listening carefully to what people find funny.
When you produce a laugh from a person or an audience, you must ask
yourself:
We are often funny for good reasons that we fail to see. As a result, we do not
produce those moments as often as you could. Finding your inherent and
intrinsic funny bone - understanding how and why it works - will allow you to
be funnier more consistently.
So pay attention.
Take note of what works for you.
Recognize why your guaranteed laugh lines are so guaranteed.
Know thyself.
Much of what you need is already inside of you.

Food

School

Music
Shopping
Nostalgia, when deployed properly, often makes people laugh because it
serves as a reminder of how different, rudimentary, and odd the world once
was. The best thing about nostalgia is that even when the joke doesn’t land,
it still serves to inform people about the past, particularly if they were too
young (or unborn) to remember.
In each of the following categories, try to find at least one example of
nostalgia that is so different, arcane, or bizarre from the world we live in
today that it might make someone laugh.
STRATEGY #1 - NOSTALGIA
Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	11

-- 11 of 17 --

Exaggeration can come in many forms, including:
1. Size
2. Degree
3. Intensity
4. Quality
5. Importance
When we exaggerate, we make it clear to the audience that our exaggeration
is being made for amusement and not accuracy.
People who exaggerate in hopes that others will believe their exaggeration
are known as politicians.
Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	12
The size of a spider
The poor condition of an automobile
A quality of a Thanksgiving Day turkey
The importance of a recent work assignment
The length of a road trip
The degree of an injury
The temperature of a room
Politicians are never funny.
Also, we should never resort to cliché exaggerations like “He’s older than the
hills” or “I’m so hungry I could eat a horse.”
As you know, clichés are always awful.
Try exaggerating the following subjects in an amusing way by using
language and/or comparison that is both original and unexpected:
STRATEGY #2 - EXAGGERATION

-- 12 of 17 --

Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	13
One of your parents
Corn on the cob
A memorable (for good or ill) teacher
Your first car
Your first kiss
A pet
Orange juice
Your greatest enemy
This is a simple game of upending expectations. Offer two descriptors on a
subject that are both aligned and expected, then make the third descriptor
as different (but still accurate) from the first two as possible.
Try to describe these things using “One of these things is not like the other.”
Remember:
Describe each of these things using two similarly expected descriptors
(usually but not always adjectives) plus one unexpected and dissimilar
descriptor.
STRATEGY #3 - ONE OF THESE
THINGS IS NOT LIKE THE OTHER

-- 13 of 17 --

Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	14
Metaphors are just like similes except that they don’t include the words “like”
or “as” to describe a subject. Instead, you simply say one thing is another
thing. They can be slightly more difficult to create but are often funnier than
similes when done well.
To create a metaphor, follow the same steps of a simile. Identify one or two
qualities from the subject of your metaphor.
Then find something else in the world that also possesses those one or two
qualities or the opposite of those qualities. The less objectively similar the
two subjects of comparison are, the more likely you will stumble upon
something funny.
In other words:
Try to distill the subject down to one or two singular attributes, then find
other subjects with the same attributes to create your metaphor.
STRATEGY #4 - METAPHOR

-- 14 of 17 --

Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	15
FOR EXAMPLE:
Public intoxication
Smoking
Ku Klux Klan robes
Swearing
Public nudity
Loud children
People talking in movie theaters
Phone
Traffic cone
Winter
Laser pointer
Spoon
Penny
Slippers
SUBJECT:
Diet Coke
ATTRIBUTE:
Diet Coke is a source of unsolicited public scorn for anyone drinking it.
COMPARISON:
What other things provoke unsolicited scorn?
METAPHOR:
Diet Coke is a loud, obnoxious child running up and down the aisles in a
movie theater. Consuming it gives everyone in the world permission to
criticize your life choices via either side-eye or direct confrontation.
Try doing the same with these subjects:

-- 15 of 17 --

STRATEGY #5 - DEFINITIONS
Window
Crossword puzzle
Couch
Cat
Refrigerator
Envelope
Glove compartment
High school gymnasium
Defining something by reducing it down to its barest, most amusing, most,
ironic, or more bizarre qualities can often be hilarious while also saying
something about you as a person (thus allowing it to do double duty).
Imagine pouring the subject of your definition into a colander and straining
out only those qualities that make it funny. Oftentimes these qualities relate
to the origin, history, purpose, physical characteristics, or limitations of the
subject.
A chair, for example, is many things, but when defined by its purpose, it
might be described as an anti-gravity ass catcher. A flimsy weapon of
violence in a low budget action film. A catcher’s mitt for your butt, which is
also a metaphor and as well as a definition because remember, everything is
everything.
Here are a few subjects to try to construct amusing definitions:
Humor Volume #1 Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks 	16

My Journey Into Storytelling
Matthew Dicks is the internationally bestselling author of the novels Memoirs of an
Imaginary Friend, Something Missing, Unexpectedly, Milo, The Perfect Comeback of
Caroline Jacobs, Twenty-one Truths About Love, and The Other Mother, and the
nonfiction titles Storyworthy: Engage, Teach, Persuade, and Change Your Life through
the Power of Storytelling and Someday Is Today: 22 Simple Actionable Ways to Propel
Your Creative Life.
His novels have been translated into more than 25 languages worldwide.
Matthew is a record 58-time Moth StorySLAM champion and 9-time GrandSLAM
champion who tells stories on stages all over the world.
Matthew’s clients have included Amazon, Slack, Johnson & Johnson, Saatchi &
Saatchi, Salesforce, MetLife, Pfizer, Repligen, Yale University, Harvard University, MIT,
and many, many more.
Matthew is the creator, producer, and co-host of Speak Up Storytelling, a podcast
that teaches people to find and tell their best stories.
For more Storytelling Resources
Visit www.Storyworthmd.com
